content:
The ICT concepts used in this module include:
- The ICT Power of Three
- Importance of developing anticipatory price skills
- Engineering liquidity in the market
- Neutralizing liquidity in the market
- Market making and pairing of orders
- What accumulation looks like in bull and bear conditions
- Where manipulation occurs in bull and bear conditions
- What distribution looks like in bull and bear conditions

section_title: UNIVERSAL APPLICABILITY OF THE POWER OF THREE
content:
A crude depiction of an Open-High-Low-Close (OHLC) bar is used. For this teaching, every individual bar
or candle is referred to as a daily range or daily bar. All concepts presented here are applicable to every
time measurement or time interval. If a market can be charted with a beginning time, highest value, lowest
value, and an ending time, this concept is applicable, regardless of the time frame (Forex, commodities,
bonds, etc.).

section_title: DEFINING THE ICT POWER OF THREE AND CHARTING PREFERENCES
content:
The ICT Power of Three is fundamentally a concept of:
- Looking for accumulation
- Waiting for manipulation
- Looking for a period of distribution
Candlesticks are preferred for visual ease, as after many years of staring at price action, they are easier on
the eye than raw OHLC bars, though the underlying concept remains the same.

section_title: THE GENESIS OF THE ICT POWER OF THREE CONCEPT
content:
The origination behind this idea was inspired by mentor Larry Williams. He remarked on wishing he knew
who the traders were that could be buying below the open or selling above the close on bullish or up-close
days. This became a personal challenge for the speaker, who spent the first quarter of 25 years mastering
this concept. It has been proven real, tangible, and repeatable not only in personal results but also by
others who have learned it.

CONTEXT)
The concept hinges on the reference points that start our time interval, represented as a daily bar for a full
24-hour period.
Four key reference points:
- Opening price: The initial value price prior to any imbalance.
- Closing price: The ending value price post price imbalance.
- Range expansion: The portion making up the range between the open and close, referred to as a
dynamic price imbalance.
In a bullish up-close example, this would be a bullish range expansion or a dynamic price imbalance,
indicating more movement to the upside seeking a higher price above the initial opening price, resulting in
a bullish close.

For the Power of Three, when we are bullish on the marketplace, we determine what is accumulating. We
look for accumulation in the form of long positions building up around the opening price. This accumulation
is performed by smart money.

When anticipating a bullish range expansion to the upside (a higher close), we hunt for a manipulation
cycle immediately after the opening price. On a daily range, if we are bullish, we want to be buying at or,
ideally, below that opening price.
We buy below the opening price because market makers look to engineer short liquidity. When the market
breaks quickly below an old low, traders view this as a breakout entry and sell short on a stop. This
engineering forces liquidity into the market to sell at a very low price. The sudden movement entices
sideline traders, causing them to sell short right before the market stops on a dime, reverses, and goes
north. This initial drop down below the opening price is significant as it engineers willing parties to sell
short, flooding the market with counterparties to smart money, who want to buy at a deep discount.

Another form of manipulation in bullish conditions is neutralizing long liquidity. This means individuals who
are already long or recently bought near the opening price have their positions upset by the drop below the
opening price. Their stop-losses are run, not to engineer them into a new lower entry, but to knock them out
of an initially well-placed position.
Two conditions occur simultaneously below the opening price when bullish:
- Knocking out individuals who would otherwise be profitable as the market rallies.
- Inducing or engineering a short-term sentiment shift to bearishness by running short-term lows. This
takes out long holders with their sell stops and puts breakout short traders on the wrong side of the market.
MANIPULATION:

Manipulation is seen in how market makers pair orders. By pairing these sell stops (from long holders and
breakout shorts) with smart money buying interest, the manipulation cycle is revealed:
- A run below the opening to accumulate long positions before the big up move.

Finally, the distribution cycle occurs where smart money pairs its long exits (selling out of their longs) with
pending buy interest. This pending buy interest comes from:
- Breakout artists buying above an old high, viewing it as buying strength. Smart money sells their long
positions to these breakout artists.
- Individuals trying to sell short intraday who might have a buy stop above an intraday high. Smart money
runs through that intraday high and sells to those buy stops. Their long exits are paired with a short-term
intraday swing high.
If there is a nice little retracement lower intraday before the close, that's where an area of distribution would
come in.

In a bearish context, we look at an Open-High-Low-Close bar with a down close. This is a crude depiction
of a daily bar, comparable to a bearish closed candle.
Again, the key reference points:
- Opening price: The initial value price prior to any imbalance.
- Closing price: The ending value price of that time interval (e.g., daily range) after the price imbalance or
movement between the open and close.
- Range expansion cycle: This is the dynamic price imbalance between the opening and closing price.
When bearish, we anticipate a lower close.

In the form of Power of Three, when we are bearish, what is accumulating? From the opening, short
positions are building up. Smart money is building up these short positions at or just above the opening
price.

When bearish, we anticipate manipulation. The form of manipulation we expect to see is engineering long
liquidity. This involves a quick, sudden movement higher to entice breakout artists who want to buy. They
will be placed on the wrong side of the marketplace.

Another form of manipulation is neutralizing short liquidity. Individuals who are already short and would
otherwise profit from the coming decline are knocked out by having their buy stops tripped. They are
neutralized in their short position.
When a buy stop is triggered, it becomes a market order to buy at the market. Smart money acts as the
counterparty, selling to those willing buy stops. While smart money sells to these buy stops (fulfilling their
buy orders), their overall sell position is intended to ride the market lower and make money.
Manipulation, in its true sense, in bearish conditions, occurs at or above the opening price where buy stops
are being paired with short interest from smart money.

Finally, at the end of the day, the distribution cycle is seen where smart money pairs its short exits
(covering their short positions) with pending sell interest. To cover a short position, smart money has to buy
it back. They will target some old low where sell stops would be resting.
Once triggered, these sell stops become market orders to sell into the marketplace, flooding it with sellers.
The best time to cover a short is when there's a flood of sellers willing to sell at a low price. This would be
the distribution cycle of the daily range.

In a bullish condition, accumulation visually appears as a consolidation around the opening price (green
shaded area). This consolidation leads into a decline.

Manipulation in a bullish condition looks like a decline below the green shaded accumulation area right
before the market starts to trade higher. Traders often perceive this decline as weakness. However, smart
money traders would be wanting to buy ahead of that decline.
The market creates this fake move, inducing the opposite mindset or sentiment. This drop down out of the
consolidation when overall bullish is manipulation. It's knocking out short-term lows, taking out long holders
who can't profit from the up move, and putting traders in on a breakout short, placing them on the wrong
side of the marketplace.

At the day's highs, we see distribution where smart money exits their long positions by selling above an old
high. Buy stops would be resting above this high. These buy stops are hit, flooding the market with buyers
at a higher price. Smart money sells their long positions to this higher buying interest in the form of their
buy stops being tagged.

In a bearish condition, accumulation typically involves a consolidation. The market then moves higher,
which constitutes the manipulation phase. For example, if there's a double top on the left side of the chart,
buy stops would reside there. Lesser-informed traders would place their buy stops above this perceived stiff
resistance price point.
However, the market will want to probe through those levels for buy stops. Once those buy stops trigger,
anyone short is now allowed to be profitable in the move going lower; their shorts are neutralized by hitting
their buy stops. Breakout artists, seeing the equal high and the move out of the consolidation, would be
buying that on a break or trying to buy strength. Both are incorrect. The market is driving higher to pair
traders to buy at a high price, while smart money short sellers sell short to those individuals who want to
buy at a high price, making a profit as the market eventually moves lower.

Towards the end of the day, the distribution cycle comes in. Short holders in the smart money camp look to
collapse their short positions. To close a short position, they have to buy it back. They will look for an area
to run below old lows. For example, 20 pips below the original consolidation, where the day's low forms, is
exactly where smart money would be collapsing their short positions for a day trade. This is the ending
cycle of distribution: smart money collapsing their shorts by buying it back at an area where sellers below
the market would be interested in selling.

section_title: COMPREHENSIVE DAILY RANGE POWER OF THREE EXAMPLE
content:
Overall, if you understand this concept, you can look at the marketplace like this:
- Accumulation (green consolidation): The market reaches down below the consolidation into an area of
liquidity (e.g., 10 pips below equal lows). Sell stops are triggered where smart money would be buying.
- Manipulation: Once those sell stops are tripped, smart money is buying below the day's open.
- Expansion: The market rallies up, experiencing an expansion move or range expansion up into a move
running (e.g., 20 pips above the old high).
- Distribution: Your distribution cycle then goes into the marketplace. This completes a full daily range using
the Power of Three concept.
